title: Valid Parenthesis

content.problemStatement:
Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.

An input string is valid if:

Open brackets must be closed using the same type of brackets.
Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.
Every close bracket has a corresponding open bracket of the same type.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string s consisting only of the characters '(' , ')' , '{' , '}' , '[' and ']'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "true" if the string is a valid parentheses sequence, otherwise output "false".

sampleTestCases.explanation: First string is perfectly nested, second mixes three types correctly, third has a mismatched closing bracket.
First string is valid across all three bracket types, second violates the required order.
A single pair of parentheses is valid.

solution.approach: Use a stack to keep track of opening brackets. Scan the string from left to right. When an opening bracket is encountered, push it onto the stack. For a closing bracket, the stack must not be empty and its top must be the matching opening bracket; otherwise the string is invalid. After processing the whole string the stack must be empty for the sequence to be valid. This greedy linear scan works because any valid parentheses sequence can be reduced by repeatedly removing adjacent matching pairs, which is exactly what the stack simulation does.
solution.timeComplexity: O(L) per test case, where L is the length of the string. Overall O(total length of all strings).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(L) in the worst case for the stack, where L is the length of the current string.

tcGeneratorPython:
def generate_case(i, RINT, POWER):
    # i: test case index (0 <= i <= 20)
    # RINT(a,b): random integer in [a,b]
    # POWER(a,b): a**b
    if i == 0:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('()')
    elif i == 1:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('(')
    elif i == 2:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print(')')
    elif i == 3:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('[]')
    elif i == 4:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('({[]})({[]})')
    elif i == 5:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print(')'*RINT(13,15))
    elif i == 6:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('(' * RINT(13,15))
    elif i == 7:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('([]{})[]{}()')
    elif i == 8:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('()'*RINT(5,7))
    elif i == 9:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('(' * RINT(4,6))
    elif i == 10:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('({[]})')
    elif i == 11:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('])}{')
    elif i == 12:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('([)]')
    elif i == 13:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('()(')
    elif i == 14:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('([]')
    elif i == 15:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('([{}])')
    elif i == 16:
        t = 3
        print(t)
        print('()')
        print('([])')
        print('([)]')
    elif i == 17:
        t = 5
        print(t)
        print('()')
        print('()[]')
        print('({})')
        print('([{}])')
        print('((()))')
    elif i == 18:
        t = 2
        print(t)
        print('({[]})')
        print('({[)]})')
    elif i == 19:
        t = 4
        print(t)
        print('[]')
        print('{}')
        print('()')
        print('([{}])')
    else:
        # default fallback
        t = 1
        print(t)
        print('()')
